steps.title: Understand what the conclusion requires
steps.content:
Mulder and Spellman concluded that invasives are advantaged by delays in subfreezing temperature onset. A finding that directly supports this must show the invasive species (P. aviculare) benefiting more than the native species (C. canadensis) when subfreezing temperatures are delayed — not just both responding, but the invasive responding to a greater degree.
  • Choice A — 'Although C. canadensis and P. aviculare tended to stop producing leaves at about the same time in years with historically typical temperature patterns, P. aviculare stopped producing leaves sooner than C. canadensis did in years with late subfreezing temperature onset.': P. aviculare stops sooner than C. canadensis under delayed onset, so this does not show an invasive advantage. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Although significant interannual variations in subfreezing temperature onset were observed during the study, neither P. aviculare nor C. canadensis showed any significant interannual variation in the cessation of leaf production.': Neither species responds to the temperature variation at all — no differential advantage can be shown. Fails to support the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Although C. canadensis and P. aviculare both tended to produce leaves later into autumn in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, the extension was much greater for P. aviculare than for C. canadensis.': Both species extend their growing season when temperatures are delayed, but the invasive extends it much more — this directly shows the invasive is advantaged. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Although C. canadensis and P. aviculare both tended to produce more leaves overall in years with late subfreezing temperature onset than they did in years with historically typical temperature patterns, the years with late subfreezing temperature onset also had early growing season onset in spring.': Introduces a confounding variable (early spring onset) that could explain the extra leaf production — the delayed autumn temperatures are not isolated as the cause. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The correct answer is the only finding that shows the invasive capitalizing on delayed subfreezing temperatures to a greater degree than the native species, directly supporting the researchers' conclusion. That is the correct answer.
